explanation: S2.1 expresses highly instrumental and self-protective reasoning, viewing relationships and rules transactionally[cite: 1]. Responses like 'Is fine as long as they aren't slowing me down' and 'Look out for number one' reflect a primary focus on fulfilling personal needs and avoiding punishment[cite: 1].

S3.1 demonstrates the Socialized Mind, heavily relying on external validation and group harmony[cite: 1]. Their identity is defined by interpersonal relationships, evidenced by worrying about 'what other people think of me' and feeling 'lost if I don't get feedback from the other teachers'[cite: 1].

S3.2 relies entirely on external authorities, such as parents and advisors, to define their goals and values[cite: 1]. They state they 'don't really know what I want unless someone tells me it's a good idea,' showing they are completely subject to the expectations of others[cite: 1].

S3.3 is deeply invested in upholding appearances and maintaining the respect of their superiors[cite: 1]. Their fear of being 'completely exposed' and taking over work to avoid letting their team make a mistake indicates their self-worth is intrinsically tied to how others perceive their competence[cite: 1].

S3.4 feels 'stuck in the middle' of conflicting interpersonal loyalties[cite: 1]. Being 'completely torn between being a good leader and a loyal friend' shows they are subject to their relationships rather than holding an independent perspective on them[cite: 1].

S4.1 exhibits the Self-Authoring Mind by filtering feedback through an internal value system[cite: 1]. They emphasize the ability to 'think for myself' and value a partner who supports growth while 'maintaining their own independent identity,' reflecting clear, self-defined internal boundaries[cite: 1].

S4.2 can step back and observe their own reactions, deliberately choosing responses based on internal criteria[cite: 1]. Their focus on acting 'in alignment with her own internal compass' rather than seeking external validation is a hallmark of Stage 4 cognitive development[cite: 1].

S4.3 authors their own principles and evaluates situations objectively to 'design better systems'[cite: 1]. They take responsibility for their own structures and boundaries, focusing on whether a strategy was inherently flawed rather than worrying about others' approval[cite: 1].

S4.4 prioritizes self-direction and evaluates rules against their 'own internal moral compass'[cite: 1]. They define their own worth internally and view relationships as connections between 'two independent adults,' demonstrating a highly self-authored identity[cite: 1].

S4.5 leads with a firm set of internal values, prioritizing systemic integrity over popularity[cite: 1]. They are comfortable standing 'firm in their convictions' and evaluate situations based on whether they have compromised their own principles, not on external friction[cite: 1].

S5.1 exhibits the Self-Transforming Mind, viewing identity and relationships as a 'continual process of mutual transformation'[cite: 1]. They actively invite critique to expose their own limitations and are fully willing to let deeply held frameworks 'be dismantled'[cite: 1].

S5.2 focuses on holding complexity and relaxing personal boundaries to allow for the co-creation of intimacy and truth[cite: 1]. They demonstrate an ability to 'let go of old versions' of themselves and embrace the ambiguity of interconnected, evolving systems[cite: 1].

S23.1 is transitioning from instrumental needs to a socialized identity rooted in team loyalty[cite: 1]. They focus on doing their duty to protect others and worry about what their 'squad mates think,' reflecting an emerging internalization of shared group values over purely personal gain[cite: 1].

S34.1 is actively fighting to establish independence from external expectations but reacts defensively to perceived threats to their autonomy[cite: 1]. They insist on judging their own performance but admit to 'getting defensive and pushing back hard' when their authority is questioned, typical of early Stage 4 development[cite: 1].

S34.2 is constructing a self-authored identity but experiences friction with their family's expectations[cite: 1]. They note they are 'finally figuring out what I actually believe' but are still heavily focused on proving their independence to others, revealing lingering Stage 3 attachments[cite: 1].

S34.3 possesses their own professional standards but struggles with the conflict it causes with leadership[cite: 1]. They know what is best for the students but feel dread and anxiety over how speaking up will affect their interpersonal relationships and perceived loyalty[cite: 1].

S34.4 has developed an independent vision but exhibits defensiveness to protect their newly formed boundaries[cite: 1]. They value independence but admit they 'can get defensive when I feel like my authority or autonomy is being questioned' by upper management[cite: 1].

S34.5 is shifting from seeking external approval to following their own convictions[cite: 1]. They try to evaluate actions against their internal values but honestly admit they 'still sometimes catch myself wanting a pat on the back' for doing the right thing[cite: 1].

S34.6 has the intellectual capacity to define their own boundaries but lacks the internal authority to enact them under pressure[cite: 1]. They state they know exactly what their boundaries should be but 'freeze the second I actually have to enforce them'[cite: 1].

S34.7 realizes they need to be the one to author their own priorities but feels 'pulled apart' by competing demands from their boss and wife[cite: 1]. They 'know I need to figure out what I want and make a choice' but feel trapped by the desire not to hurt anybody, demonstrating a struggle to fully self-author[cite: 1].

S45.1 has a strong self-authored foundation but is beginning to see its limitations and the value of opposing viewpoints[cite: 1]. They try to treat feedback as 'data about a competing, valid way of seeing the world' but note they sometimes still 'catch myself treating my own subjective perspective as an objective truth'[cite: 1].

S45.2 is developing the capacity to hold multiple perspectives and question their own frameworks[cite: 1]. They strive to hold convictions loosely but acknowledge a 'lingering instinct to defend my own well-crafted arguments' instead of immediately remaining open to conflicting data[cite: 1].

S45.3 actively deconstructs their own certainties and finds immense joy in being 'profoundly wrong'[cite: 1]. They recognize the limits of theoretical models but humbly admit they still catch themselves trying to force a 'messy, beautiful reality into one of my neat theoretical boxes'[cite: 1].
